h1Title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS): Symptoms, Causes & Treatment
heroOverview: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a progressive neurodegenerative disease of both upper and lower motor neurons that leads to loss of voluntary muscle control throughout the body. It affects approximately 2 per 100,000 people annually, with a lifetime risk of about 1 in 400; roughly 30,000 people are living with ALS in the United States at any given time. The disease typically begins with focal weakness or fasciculations in one limb, progresses relentlessly over months to years, and ultimately impairs swallowing, speech, and breathing. Median survival from symptom onset is 2-4 years, though approximately 10% of patients survive beyond 10 years. Two FDA-approved disease-modifying drugs — riluzole and edaravone — modestly slow progression; tofersen has extended this armamentarium for SOD1-ALS. Supportive care including non-invasive ventilation extends survival significantly.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 5.0 per 100,000 persons prevalence in European populations (Logroscino et al. 2018); approximately 30,000 prevalent US cases at any time (ALS Association 2023)
keyStats.demographics: Men affected 1.3-2× more often than women; Caucasian populations have higher rates than African or Asian populations in most studies; approximately 5-10% are familial
keyStats.avgAge: Median age of onset approximately 55-65 years for sporadic ALS; familial cases present a decade earlier; juvenile ALS (onset below 25 years) is rare
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 222,801 prevalent cases globally in 2015 (GBD 2016 study, PMID 28404217); projections suggest global prevalence will rise to approximately 376,674 by 2040 due to aging populations

definition: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ICD-10: G12.21), also known as Lou Gehrig's disease in North America and motor neurone disease (MND) in the UK and Australia, is a fatal neurodegenerative disorder defined by the progressive degeneration and death of both upper motor neurons (UMN) in the primary motor cortex and lower motor neurons (LMN) in the anterior horn of the spinal cord and motor nuclei of the brainstem. 'Amyotrophic' refers to the muscle wasting and fasciculations from lower motor neuron loss; 'lateral sclerosis' describes the hardening (gliosis) of the lateral corticospinal tracts from upper motor neuron degeneration. The El Escorial criteria (1994, revised Awaji criteria 2008) require UMN and LMN signs in at least two of four regions (bulbar, cervical, thoracic, lumbar) for definite diagnosis. Pathologically, ALS is characterized by cytoplasmic aggregates of TDP-43 protein in approximately 97% of cases; SOD1 and FUS mutations produce distinct aggregate patterns. Approximately 5-10% of cases are familial (FALS), with C9orf72 hexanucleotide repeat expansion (causing 20-40% of FALS and 5-7% of sporadic ALS), SOD1 (10-20% of FALS), FUS (3-5%), and TARDBP mutations as the dominant genetic causes. Approximately 90-95% of cases are sporadic (SALS) with no identifiable genetic cause. ALS is managed by specialist multidisciplinary neurology teams including neurologists, respiratory physicians, speech and language therapists, dietitians, physiotherapists, and palliative care specialists.

typesClassification.description: Approximately 90-95% of cases, with no family history or identifiable causative mutation. Median onset age 55-65. Most cases show TDP-43 proteinopathy histologically. Some sporadic cases carry de novo mutations in known ALS genes.

Approximately 5-10% of cases with a positive family history and identifiable gene mutation in the majority. Onset typically a decade earlier than sporadic ALS. C9orf72, SOD1, FUS, and TARDBP are the most common genes. Genetic counseling is essential.
About 65-70% of cases. Begins with unilateral arm or leg weakness, often with mixed UMN and LMN signs. Spreads contiguously to adjacent regions. Median survival from onset approximately 2-4 years.

About 25-30% of cases, more common in older women. Presents with slurred speech (dysarthria) and swallowing difficulty (dysphagia) before limb symptoms. Median survival from onset approximately 2-3 years due to earlier respiratory compromise.

A pure upper-motor-neuron variant with no clinical lower motor neuron signs for at least four years. Median survival substantially longer than ALS — often greater than 20 years. Reclassified as ALS if LMN signs emerge after the four-year threshold.

primarySymptoms: Unilateral, progressive limb weakness beginning in one hand, foot, or proximal limb — typically painless — causing dropping of objects, foot-drop, or difficulty climbing stairs as the first presenting complaint in 65-70% of cases.

Fasciculations — visible, irregular flickering muscle twitches under the skin — occurring widely across the body from lower motor neuron denervation; a key physical examination finding supporting the diagnosis.
Muscle cramps, often at night, occurring before significant weakness is apparent; among the earliest symptoms in some patients and reflecting lower motor neuron hyperexcitability.
Slurred, nasal, or spastic speech (dysarthria) from upper and lower motor neuron involvement of the bulbar musculature; ranges from mild imprecision to complete anarthria.
Difficulty swallowing (dysphagia) with choking on liquids before solids in UMN-predominant bulbar disease, or saliva pooling and food sticking in LMN-predominant bulbar disease.
Progressive leg spasticity, brisk reflexes, and a positive Babinski sign from corticospinal tract degeneration — UMN signs that distinguish ALS from pure lower motor neuron diseases.

Emotional lability — episodes of uncontrollable laughing or crying disproportionate to the emotional stimulus — from pseudobulbar palsy in bulbar and cervical ALS; affects approximately 20-50% of patients.

Muscle atrophy and visible wasting most prominent in the intrinsic hand muscles (first dorsal interosseous, thenar eminence) and tongue, developing over weeks to months from lower motor neuron degeneration.

Progressive breathlessness on exertion or when lying flat (orthopnea), from diaphragmatic and intercostal muscle weakening; FVC decline below 50% predicted is a clinical milestone triggering ventilation discussions.

Frontotemporal cognitive or behavioral changes — apathy, disinhibition, word-finding difficulty — in approximately 15% of ALS patients who meet criteria for ALS-frontotemporal dementia (ALS-FTD), most commonly with C9orf72 mutations.
earlyWarningSigns: Unilateral hand clumsiness or progressive difficulty with fine motor tasks (buttoning, writing, opening jars) in a middle-aged adult without cervical cord compression or peripheral nerve cause.
Isolated foot-drop causing a steppage gait, which is frequently misattributed to lumbar disc disease before ALS signs in other regions emerge.
Involuntary jerky emotional responses (pathological laughing or crying) with normal MRI brain and without prior brain injury — an early sign of pseudobulbar palsy in bulbar ALS.

Diffuse or widespread fasciculations persisting for more than three to four weeks, particularly when occurring outside the area of clinical weakness, which distinguishes ALS fasciculations from benign fasciculation syndrome.
emergencySigns: Acute respiratory failure with paradoxical abdominal movement, oxygen saturation below 90%, and accessory muscle use in a patient with known or suspected ALS — requires urgent non-invasive ventilation and ICU-level assessment.
Acute aspiration episode with cyanosis, desaturation, and inability to clear the airway — risk of aspiration pneumonia, which is the leading proximate cause of death in ALS.
Sudden inability to swallow saliva or medications in a patient with progressive bulbar ALS — requires urgent dietitian and speech therapy assessment, nasogastric tube consideration, and PEG planning.

New rapid deterioration of respiratory function (FVC fall of more than 10% in one month) or onset of morning headache and hypersomnolence from nocturnal hypoventilation — indicates urgent need for non-invasive ventilation.

causes.description: A pathological expansion (usually greater than 30 repeats) in the non-coding region of the C9orf72 gene is the single most common genetic cause of ALS, accounting for 20-40% of familial ALS and 5-7% of sporadic ALS in European populations. Causes both ALS and frontotemporal dementia, often in the same family. Toxic gain-of-function from RNA foci and dipeptide repeat proteins drive neurodegeneration.

Over 200 point mutations in SOD1, encoding copper-zinc superoxide dismutase, cause 10-20% of familial ALS and 1-2% of sporadic ALS. Mutant SOD1 protein misfolds and aggregates, causing toxic gain-of-function neurodegeneration. The clinical phenotype varies: D90A is a common slow-progressing European variant; A4V is aggressive with median survival under 12 months.

causes.cause: TDP-43 proteinopathy (TARDBP mutations and sporadic TDP-43 aggregation)
causes.description: Nuclear TDP-43 (TAR DNA-binding protein 43) clears the nucleus and forms cytoplasmic aggregates in approximately 97% of ALS cases, including most sporadic cases. Mutations in TARDBP cause approximately 3-5% of familial ALS. TDP-43 loss of nuclear function and cytoplasmic toxicity both contribute to motor neuron death.

Mutations in the FUS RNA-binding protein gene account for 3-5% of familial ALS, particularly in juvenile-onset cases. FUS and TDP-43 share structural and functional properties; FUS mutations cause cytoplasmic aggregation through nuclear export defects.

Excessive glutamate signaling through NMDA and AMPA receptors overwhelms the antioxidant and excitotoxicity defenses of motor neurons. Impaired glutamate reuptake by astroglial EAAT2 transporters is observed in spinal cord tissue from ALS patients. Riluzole's modest disease-modifying effect works primarily through sodium-channel blockade and reduction of glutamate release.

Activated microglia, astrocytes releasing pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α, IL-6), and defective phagocytosis of damaged neurons amplify motor neuron death. Astrocytes from ALS patients are directly toxic to motor neurons in co-culture experiments.

riskFactors.factor: Family history and ALS gene mutations
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of FALS patients carry a substantially elevated risk. C9orf72 expansion has autosomal dominant inheritance with incomplete penetrance. Genetic testing is recommended for all familial cases and offered to sporadic cases given that 10% of apparently sporadic ALS has an identifiable mutation.

Incidence rises sharply after age 40, peaks at 55-65, and then declines slightly in the very elderly — thought to reflect the intersection of accumulated molecular damage and a selective survival bias.

Men have 1.3-2× higher incidence than women in most epidemiological studies. The sex difference narrows after age 70. Estrogen's proposed neuroprotective effects and differences in occupational exposure contribute to this disparity.

Veterans have approximately 2× higher ALS incidence than the general population in several US studies (PMID 12873418). Proposed explanations include pesticide and heavy metal exposure, intense physical exertion, traumatic injury, and battlefield stress — no single factor has been confirmed.

Pooled analysis of 5 prospective cohort studies found current smokers have approximately 1.5× relative risk of ALS (Wang et al. 2011, PMID 21282098). Oxidative stress and cytotoxins in tobacco smoke are proposed mechanisms.

Occupational lead exposure is associated with a 1.5-2× elevated ALS risk in some studies. Welding, agricultural pesticide use, and certain industrial chemical exposures have also been implicated in epidemiological analyses, though causality is difficult to establish.

Professional football players (Italian and American football), soccer players, and elite athletes have reported higher ALS rates, raising hypotheses about repetitive trauma, intense exercise-induced oxidative stress, or pesticide use on sports fields. The absolute risk elevation remains small.

affectedDemographics: White European and North American populations have higher incidence rates (approximately 3-5 per 100,000 per year) compared with Asian and African populations, likely reflecting a combination of genetic background and ascertainment differences.
Men aged 55-65 years are the most commonly affected demographic; peak onset is approximately five years earlier in men than in women.
Familial ALS patients typically present approximately 10 years earlier (mean age 45-55) than sporadic ALS patients due to inherited mutations with earlier penetrance.
C9orf72 repeat expansion carriers have a higher rate of concurrent frontotemporal dementia (approximately 15-20%), which significantly affects prognosis and care planning.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of ALS is clinical, based on the demonstration of progressive upper and lower motor neuron signs in multiple body regions. There is no diagnostic biomarker, and the El Escorial/Awaji criteria provide a framework that classifies diagnostic certainty as possible, probable, or definite based on the number of affected body regions. The average delay from symptom onset to diagnosis remains 10-16 months in published series — a significant problem given the rapid pace of disease — largely because early focal presentations mimic cervical or lumbar radiculopathy, multifocal motor neuropathy, Kennedy disease, or inclusion body myositis. Neurological examination by an ALS-experienced clinician is the cornerstone: the combination of UMN signs (spasticity, hyperreflexia, Babinski sign) with LMN signs (atrophy, fasciculations, hyporeflexia) in the same region is the essential diagnostic finding. Electromyography (EMG) and nerve conduction studies (NCS) are the most important ancillary investigations, demonstrating active and chronic denervation across multiple myotomes (C9orf72-phenotype) and normal sensory nerve conduction. MRI of the brain and spinal cord excludes structural compressive or demyelinating mimics — dual-echo MRI may show T2 hyperintensity of the corticospinal tract in ALS, though this finding is neither sensitive nor specific. Blood tests include CK (mildly elevated in 50-75% of cases from muscle denervation), copper and vitamin B12 to exclude nutritional myelopathy, GM1 antibodies to exclude multifocal motor neuropathy, and genetic testing for C9orf72, SOD1, FUS, and TARDBP in all patients regardless of family history. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis is normal in ALS and is performed only to exclude inflammatory or infectious mimics. Spirometry (FVC, SNIP) at baseline establishes respiratory baseline and guides ventilation timing. Cognitive and behavioral assessment with validated tools (ALS-CBS, Edinburgh Cognitive and Behavioral ALS Screen) screens for frontotemporal involvement.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Most important ancillary test; demonstrates active denervation (fibrillations, positive sharp waves) and chronic reinnervation across multiple non-contiguous myotomes; normal sensory nerve conduction distinguishes ALS from peripheral neuropathies
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Small needle electrodes are inserted into representative muscles throughout the body. Mild discomfort similar to a sharp pinch for each insertion. Typically takes 60-90 minutes. Performed by a clinical neurophysiologist; results interpreted same day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes structural mimics (cervical myelopathy, foramen magnum lesions, demyelinating disease); may show corticospinal tract T2 hyperintensity supporting the diagnosis in equivocal cases
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-60 minutes in the MRI scanner; no radiation; claustrophobic patients may need mild sedation. Contrast injection in specific protocols. Results read by a neuroradiologist within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Pulmonary function tests (FVC, SNIP, peak cough flow)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies respiratory muscle weakness at diagnosis; FVC below 75% predicted or SNIP below 40 cmH2O at diagnosis predicts earlier need for non-invasive ventilation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blowing into a spirometer while seated; then nasal sniff pressure measured by a small clip in the nostril. Takes 20-30 minutes. Repeated every three months to track respiratory decline.

diagnosticTests.test: Genetic testing panel (C9orf72, SOD1, FUS, TARDBP, others)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies hereditary ALS mutations in familial and sporadic cases; essential for confirming SOD1-ALS (for tofersen eligibility) and for family counseling
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results from a laboratory specializing in neurogenetic testing. Results take two to six weeks. Requires pre- and post-test genetic counseling.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum creatine kinase (CK), complete blood count, metabolic panel, copper, B12, GM1 antibodies
diagnosticTests.purpose: Mildly elevated CK supports motor neuron disease; excludes treatable mimics including multifocal motor neuropathy (GM1 antibodies), copper deficiency myelopathy, and Kennedy disease (androgen receptor CAG repeat)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Multiple blood draws from a single venipuncture. GM1 antibodies can be sent to a neuroimmunology reference laboratory. Results within one to five days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Objectively documents aspiration risk and pharyngeal function in bulbar ALS; guides decisions on diet modification, compensatory swallowing strategies, and PEG timing
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Videofluoroscopy involves swallowing barium-mixed food under X-ray; FEES involves passing a flexible endoscope via the nostril. Both take 20-30 minutes in a specialist setting. No sedation required.

treatmentOverview: ALS treatment has two parallel aims: slowing disease progression with the available disease-modifying agents, and maximizing function and quality of life through multidisciplinary supportive care. Riluzole (50 mg twice daily), the first approved therapy (FDA 1995, NICE TA20), inhibits glutamate release and extends median survival by approximately two to three months. Edaravone (IV infusion 60 mg/day for 10 days on, 14 days off), FDA-approved in 2017, provides a further modest benefit in some patients — particularly those with early-stage, rapidly declining disease, though pivotal trial evidence is largely from Japanese populations. Tofersen (100 mg intrathecal injection monthly), FDA-approved 2023 for SOD1-mutant ALS, reduces neurofilament light chain (NfL, a biomarker of neuronal injury) and preliminary data suggest it slows progression; it is the first gene-target-specific therapy for ALS. Non-invasive ventilation (NIV) with bilevel positive airway pressure (BiPAP) extends survival by three to seven months and significantly improves quality of life, fatigue, and sleep; it is offered when FVC falls below 50% or when respiratory symptoms emerge. Invasive ventilation (tracheostomy and mechanical ventilation) can extend life years but with major implications for communication and care burden, and requires careful patient-led decision-making. Nutritional support via percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) or radiologically inserted gastrostomy (RIG) is recommended before FVC falls below 50% predicted, as procedural risk rises with respiratory compromise; gastrostomy does not necessarily extend life but prevents weight loss and aspiration. Speech and language therapy, augmentative and alternative communication devices (AAC, eye-tracking devices), and specialist physiotherapy are integral to the multidisciplinary care model endorsed by NICE guideline NG42 (2016) and EFNS/ENS ALS guidelines. Palliative care integration from the time of diagnosis reduces symptom burden and improves end-of-life decision-making.

medicalTreatments.name: Riluzole (50 mg twice daily orally)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line disease-modifying therapy; inhibits presynaptic glutamate release and voltage-gated sodium channels in motor neurons. Initiated at diagnosis and continued unless tolerability is a problem. Liver function monitoring monthly for six months then every three months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Extends median survival by approximately 2-3 months (Cochrane review, PMID 23235617); reduces risk of progression to mechanical ventilation in pooled analyses; most benefit in bulbar-onset ALS

medicalTreatments.name: Edaravone (60 mg IV infusion for 10 days in a 14-day cycle)
medicalTreatments.description: Free radical scavenger that reduces oxidative neuronal damage; approved by FDA 2017 and Japan PMDA based on positive Phase 3 trial in selected early-stage ALS patients (PMID 27346520). Patient eligibility requires ALS-FRS-R above 2 in all items, FVC above 80%, and disease duration under two years. Oral form (Radicava ORS) approved 2022.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Slowed ALSFRS-R decline by approximately 33% versus placebo over six months in the positive Japanese trial; benefit primarily in a predefined subgroup; studies in broader populations less consistent

medicalTreatments.name: Tofersen (100 mg intrathecal injection monthly after loading doses)
medicalTreatments.description: Antisense oligonucleotide (ASO) that reduces SOD1 mRNA and protein; approved FDA April 2023 for SOD1-mutant ALS. Reduces plasma neurofilament light chain (NfL) significantly. The VALOR trial primary endpoint (ALSFRS-R) was not met but pre-specified biomarker and exploratory clinical analyses support a disease-modifying effect.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Plasma NfL reduced by approximately 60% versus placebo (PMID 35390285); ALSFRS-R primary endpoint marginally missed but open-label extension data suggest ongoing benefit; long-term efficacy under evaluation

medicalTreatments.name: Non-invasive ventilation — bilevel positive airway pressure (BiPAP)
medicalTreatments.description: Mask-based positive-pressure ventilation used nocturnally initially and extended to daytime as respiratory failure progresses. Offered when FVC falls below 50% predicted, orthopnea develops, or nocturnal hypoventilation is detected (morning headache, somnolence). Extends survival 3-7 months and improves quality of life.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Extends survival by median 7 months versus no ventilation in a randomized trial (Bourke et al. Lancet Neurology 2006, PMID 16632309); most benefit in normal or mildly impaired bulbar function

medicalTreatments.name: Baclofen or tizanidine for spasticity
medicalTreatments.description: Muscle relaxants to reduce painful spasticity from UMN degeneration. Baclofen 5-20 mg three times daily or tizanidine 4-8 mg three times daily; intrathecal baclofen pump for refractory spasticity. Must be titrated cautiously to avoid worsening weakness.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinically meaningful reduction in spasticity and cramp frequency; improved walking in some early-stage patients

medicalTreatments.name: Nuedexta (dextromethorphan 20 mg / quinidine 10 mg twice daily) for pseudobulbar affect
medicalTreatments.description: FDA-approved for pseudobulbar affect (pathological laughing or crying) in ALS. Quinidine inhibits CYP2D6 metabolism of dextromethorphan, increasing its central effect via NMDA receptor and sigma-1 antagonism.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pseudobulbar affect center for neurologic studies scale score improved by approximately 47% versus placebo; affects 20-50% of ALS patients with bulbar involvement (PMID 20826719)

surgicalOptions.name: Percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG)
surgicalOptions.description: A feeding tube placed endoscopically through the abdominal wall into the stomach, allowing tube feeding to maintain nutrition when swallowing is unsafe or insufficient. Planned electively before FVC falls below 50%, while the patient can safely undergo sedation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Safe in approximately 95% of patients with FVC above 50%; nutritional status maintained or improved; does not significantly extend survival but prevents aspiration and weight loss

surgicalOptions.name: Radiologically inserted gastrostomy (RIG)
surgicalOptions.description: Alternative to PEG for patients with advanced respiratory compromise who cannot safely undergo endoscopic sedation. Fluoroscopically guided insertion under local anesthesia and light sedation; acceptable safety profile even with low FVC.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Comparable nutritional outcomes to PEG; complication rates slightly higher than PEG in some centers but preferred when FVC is below 50%

surgicalOptions.name: Tracheostomy and invasive mechanical ventilation
surgicalOptions.description: Permanent tracheostomy with long-term mechanical ventilation allows indefinite life extension when NIV becomes insufficient. Requires full-time care; most patients retain cognition but lose all speech. Patient preference and advance directive guidance are essential before this decision.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Extends life for variable periods (months to years); 5-10% of ALS patients opt for invasive ventilation in Western countries; rate is higher in Japan (approximately 30%)

alternativeTreatments.name: High-calorie dietary supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: Observational data from the EMPOWER trial and other studies suggest that body weight maintenance and even weight gain in ALS is associated with slower disease progression; high-fat, high-calorie diets are under investigation in clinical trials. Dietitian guidance is standard of care.

alternativeTreatments.name: Resistance and aerobic exercise (moderate intensity)
alternativeTreatments.description: Moderate resistance exercise within tolerance does not accelerate ALS progression (PMID 23954974) and may reduce cramps and maintain function transiently. High-intensity exercise is avoided. Physiotherapist-supervised programs are recommended.

whySeeSpecialist: ALS must be diagnosed and managed by a neurologist with specific expertise in motor neuron disease, ideally in a dedicated ALS multidisciplinary clinic. Multidisciplinary care is associated with significantly longer survival — median 7.5 months additional compared with non-specialist care in a retrospective study — and better quality of life.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a neurologist affiliated with a specialist ALS or motor neuron disease clinic that includes respiratory medicine, speech and language therapy, dietetics, physiotherapy, occupational therapy, and palliative care. ALS Association-recognized centers of excellence in the US, and NHS specialist neurology centers in the UK, provide the highest standard of multidisciplinary care.
hospitalCriteria: Specialist ALS or MND multidisciplinary clinic (neurology, respiratory, SLT, dietetics, OT, physiotherapy, palliative care)
On-site or networked respiratory unit for non-invasive ventilation initiation and monitoring
Interventional radiology or endoscopy suite for PEG or RIG insertion
Neurogenetics laboratory or referral pathway for comprehensive ALS gene panel testing
Augmentative and alternative communication (AAC) assessment and provision service
keyFacilities: Specialist ALS multidisciplinary clinics (ALS Association certified centers)
Neurology departments with motor neuron disease subspecialty expertise
Palliative care and hospice services experienced in progressive neurological disease
Home mechanical ventilation services for domiciliary NIV
Community nursing and ALS care coordinator services
preventionStrategies: Cease tobacco smoking: current smokers have approximately 1.5× higher ALS risk; smoking cessation reduces this excess risk.
Minimize occupational exposures to lead, pesticides, and heavy metals in high-risk industries; use personal protective equipment when such exposures are unavoidable.

Individuals with family history of ALS should undergo genetic counseling and testing; identifying a causative mutation allows participation in presymptomatic trials and informed reproductive decision-making.
Maintain regular exercise and avoid extreme physical overtraining — though evidence for exercise as an ALS prevention strategy is weak, overall neurological health benefits are well established.

Research enrolling pre-symptomatic mutation carriers (e.g., PREVENT-ALS, ATLAS) aims to slow or prevent ALS with tofersen and other ASOs before symptom onset; carrier identification opens access to these opportunities.
lifestyleModifications: Accept and use assistive devices (ankle-foot orthoses, power wheelchair, communication devices) early rather than late — maintaining independence longer with appropriate support improves function and quality of life.
Work with a registered dietitian to maintain body weight and caloric intake; weight loss predicts faster disease progression, and early nutritional intervention slows this.
Schedule respiratory monitoring (FVC every three months) and discuss NIV proactively before FVC reaches 50% to allow unhurried decision-making.
Engage advance care planning and advance directive discussions with the ALS care team and family early in the disease course, while communication is intact.

Participate in ALS research registries and clinical trials — many disease-modifying agents and symptomatic therapies are evaluated in patients at all stages, and ALS research depends on participant enrollment.
Connect with an ALS patient organization (ALS Association, MND Association UK) for peer support, equipment loan, and access to a care coordinator.

recommended: High-calorie, high-fat diet (aiming to achieve caloric surplus of 500-1,000 kcal/day above usual intake) to counteract hypermetabolism and weight loss
Calorie-dense liquid nutritional supplements between meals if solid food intake declines
Modified texture foods and thickened fluids as directed by speech and language therapist when dysphagia is present
Adequate protein intake (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) to support remaining muscle mass
avoid: Foods with a high aspiration risk (thin liquids, dry crumbly foods) when dysphagia is present — follow SLT texture modification guidance
Prolonged periods of fasting or severe caloric restriction, which accelerate muscle wasting and weight loss
Alcohol above minimal amounts in patients on riluzole (liver enzyme elevation risk) or edaravone
Dietary supplements that interact with riluzole's cytochrome P450 metabolism without medical review

exerciseGuidelines: Physiotherapist-supervised moderate resistance and aerobic exercise within tolerance is safe in early and middle-stage ALS and does not accelerate disease progression. Exercise goals shift over time from maintaining strength to maintaining function and preventing contractures. Stretching is important for managing spasticity. Hydrotherapy in a warm pool is well tolerated and beneficial for mobility and psychological well-being. All exercise plans should be individually tailored with regular reassessment.
dailyManagement: Take riluzole 50 mg twice daily at consistent times, 30-60 minutes before meals, to maximize absorption; avoid grapefruit juice, which inhibits its metabolism.
Use prescribed NIV device nightly; report excessive morning headaches, somnolence, or mask discomfort to the respiratory team for mask-fitting adjustment.
Attend specialist ALS clinic every two to three months for multidisciplinary review including respiratory function, weight, communication, and mobility assessment.
Communicate daily care preferences with family caregivers and the ALS care team; update advance directives as disease progresses.
Use AAC devices and communication apps proactively — voice banking before significant dysarthria develops preserves a natural voice for later text-to-speech use.
Report new symptoms — choking episodes, morning headache, orthopnea, sudden FVC change — to the ALS team promptly, as these signal clinical milestones requiring intervention.
prognosis: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis progresses relentlessly in virtually all patients. Median survival from symptom onset is 2-4 years; from diagnosis, approximately 1.5-2.5 years. Approximately 10% of patients survive beyond 10 years, and a small number (including Stephen Hawking) survive for decades — these outliers are disproportionately associated with early-onset limb-predominant disease or PLS reclassification. Bulbar-onset ALS carries a worse prognosis (median 18-24 months from onset) than limb-onset disease (median 30-48 months) due to earlier respiratory compromise. Multidisciplinary clinic attendance is associated with a 7.5-month survival extension in retrospective data. NIV extends survival three to seven months. Riluzole adds approximately two to three months. Tofersen for SOD1-ALS and edaravone in selected patients offer additional but modest benefit. The main causes of death are respiratory failure (approximately 70-80%), pneumonia from aspiration, and, in patients on invasive ventilation, complications of prolonged mechanical ventilation. Cognitive involvement (ALS-FTD) is a negative prognostic factor for survival and significantly increases caregiver burden.
recoveryTimeline: ALS has no recovery trajectory in the conventional sense — it is a progressive terminal condition. However, rate of progression varies widely; ALSFRS-R decline of greater than 1.0 point per month predicts median survival below 18 months. Respiratory milestones (NIV initiation, PEG insertion, FVC at 50% and 25%) are used clinically as disease waypoints for care planning.
complications: Respiratory failure from progressive diaphragm and intercostal muscle weakness, the proximate cause of death in approximately 70-80% of ALS patients who do not elect invasive ventilation.
Aspiration pneumonia from pharyngeal and laryngeal muscle weakness, particularly in bulbar-onset disease; recurrent pneumonia accelerates respiratory decline.
Malnutrition and weight loss from dysphagia and hypermetabolism; pre-morbid body weight loss greater than 5% predicts faster ALSFRS-R decline in multiple cohort studies.
Communication loss and social isolation as dysarthria progresses; proactive AAC provision and voice banking prevent total communication failure.
Frontotemporal dementia in approximately 15% of ALS patients, primarily those with C9orf72 mutation; significantly increases care burden and impairs informed decision-making.
Depression and anxiety affecting approximately 25-50% of patients and caregivers; early psychological support and pharmacotherapy reduce suffering during the disease course.

supportResources.name: ALS Association (US)
supportResources.description: The leading US ALS patient organization; provides care coordination, equipment loan, clinical trial matching, and advocacy. Certifies ALS care centers nationwide.

supportResources.name: Motor Neurone Disease Association (UK)
supportResources.description: UK charity providing specialist MND care coordinators, advice lines, equipment grants, and research funding. Operates a helpline at 0808 802 6262.

supportResources.name: Prize4Life and Target ALS Foundation
supportResources.description: Research-focused organization accelerating ALS biomarker and treatment development; provides information on open clinical trials.

supportResources.name: ClinicalTrials.gov ALS search
supportResources.description: Registry of current ALS clinical trials worldwide; filterable by location, stage, and eligibility criteria.

confusedWithConditions.name: Multiple sclerosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Multiple sclerosis produces a combination of UMN and sensory deficits with characteristic MRI white-matter lesions (McDonald criteria); it rarely causes significant lower motor neuron signs, fasciculations, or muscle wasting. ALS has no sensory involvement and normal spinal cord and brain MRI (except possibly corticospinal tract signal). Age of onset also differs: MS peaks at 20-40 years, ALS at 55-65.

confusedWithConditions.name: Primary lateral sclerosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PLS is a pure upper-motor-neuron syndrome with spasticity, hyperreflexia, and pseudobulbar palsy but no clinical LMN signs. ALS requires both UMN and LMN signs. If LMN signs emerge within four years, the diagnosis is reclassified as ALS. PLS has a substantially better prognosis than ALS, often with survival exceeding twenty years.

confusedWithConditions.name: Myasthenia gravis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Myasthenia gravis causes fatigable weakness of ocular, bulbar, and proximal limb muscles from autoimmune impairment of the neuromuscular junction — not motor neuron degeneration. Reflexes are preserved; there are no fasciculations or UMN signs. AChR or MuSK antibody testing and repetitive nerve stimulation distinguish the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Parkinson's disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Parkinson's disease causes resting tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, and postural instability from dopaminergic loss in the substantia nigra — not from motor neuron degeneration. Upper and lower motor neuron signs, fasciculations, and muscle wasting do not occur in uncomplicated Parkinson's. ALS can present with slowed movements that superficially resemble parkinsonism before atrophy and fasciculations become prominent.

relatedConditions.name: Primary lateral sclerosis
relatedConditions.relevance: PLS is a related motor neuron disease with pure UMN involvement; ALS and PLS may be part of the same disease spectrum, as PLS patients may transition to ALS when LMN signs emerge.

relatedConditions.name: Progressive bulbar palsy
relatedConditions.relevance: Progressive bulbar palsy is a motor neuron disease variant with predominant bulbar LMN involvement (dysphagia, dysarthria); most cases eventually develop limb involvement and are reclassified as bulbar-onset ALS.

relatedConditions.name: Spinal muscular atrophy
relatedConditions.relevance: SMA is a pure lower motor neuron disease from SMN1 mutations; like ALS it causes progressive motor weakness and atrophy, but lacks UMN signs and has distinct genetic causes and therapeutic options.

relatedConditions.name: Multiple sclerosis
relatedConditions.relevance: MS is a key differential diagnosis for ALS in the early phase when UMN signs predominate; MRI and absence of LMN signs in MS differentiate the two.

relatedConditions.name: Neuropathic pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Although ALS is primarily a motor disease, muscle cramps and some patients experience pain from spasticity and immobility; pain management is part of multidisciplinary ALS care.

faqs.question: What is ALS (amyotrophic lateral sclerosis)?
faqs.answer: ALS is a progressive neurodegenerative disease that destroys the motor neurons controlling voluntary movement — both in the brain (upper motor neurons) and in the spinal cord (lower motor neurons). Over months to years it causes progressive muscle weakness, atrophy, and eventually respiratory failure. There is no cure; two approved drugs modestly slow its progression.

faqs.question: What are the first signs of ALS?
faqs.answer: The most common first sign is painless weakness or clumsiness in one limb — a hand that cannot grip firmly, or a foot that drops causing trips. Muscle cramps and visible twitches (fasciculations) often appear early. Slurred speech or choking on liquids is the initial symptom in approximately 25-30% of patients (bulbar onset). Symptoms are initially one-sided.

faqs.question: How long do people live with ALS?
faqs.answer: Median survival from symptom onset is 2-4 years, and from diagnosis approximately 1.5-2.5 years. About 10% of patients survive beyond 10 years. Bulbar-onset ALS has a shorter prognosis than limb-onset ALS. Non-invasive ventilation extends survival by three to seven months; invasive ventilation can extend life for years but requires around-the-clock care.

faqs.question: Is ALS hereditary?
faqs.answer: Approximately 5-10% of ALS cases are familial, with mutations in C9orf72, SOD1, FUS, or TARDBP being the most common. C9orf72 repeat expansion also accounts for 5-7% of apparently sporadic cases. Genetic testing is now recommended for all ALS patients regardless of family history. First-degree relatives of familial ALS patients can access genetic counseling.

faqs.question: What is the difference between ALS and motor neurone disease?
faqs.answer: Motor neurone disease (MND) is the umbrella term used in the UK and Australia for a group of related conditions; ALS is the most common variant, accounting for approximately 70-80% of MND cases. Other MND subtypes include primary lateral sclerosis and progressive bulbar palsy. In common clinical usage, MND and ALS are often used interchangeably.

faqs.question: Does ALS cause pain?
faqs.answer: ALS primarily causes weakness rather than pain, but muscle cramps, spasticity, and joint discomfort from immobility can be painful in up to 70% of patients. Advanced ALS may cause shoulder pain from shoulder subluxation and pressure sores. Pain is an underrecognized symptom; multimodal pain management including baclofen, quinine, and physiotherapy is part of comprehensive ALS care.

faqs.question: What medications slow ALS progression?
faqs.answer: Riluzole (50 mg twice daily) extends median survival approximately 2-3 months and is the standard first-line disease-modifying treatment. Edaravone (intravenous or oral) slows ALSFRS-R decline in selected early-stage patients. Tofersen, an antisense oligonucleotide, is FDA-approved for SOD1-mutant ALS and significantly reduces biomarkers of neuronal injury.

faqs.question: How is ALS diagnosed?
faqs.answer: ALS is diagnosed clinically by a neurologist based on finding both upper and lower motor neuron signs in multiple body regions. EMG and nerve conduction studies confirm denervation across multiple limb and bulbar muscles. MRI brain and spine exclude other causes. Blood tests include CK and genetic panel. Average time from symptom onset to diagnosis is 10-16 months.

faqs.question: What causes ALS?
faqs.answer: In familial cases, mutations in C9orf72, SOD1, FUS, TARDBP and other genes cause motor neuron degeneration through mechanisms including protein aggregation, RNA processing defects, excitotoxicity, and oxidative stress. In sporadic ALS, the same molecular pathways are implicated but the trigger is unknown. Environmental factors (smoking, military service, pesticide exposure) modestly increase risk.

faqs.question: What is the ALSFRS-R scale?
faqs.answer: The ALS Functional Rating Scale Revised (ALSFRS-R) is a 48-point scale measuring function across speech, swallowing, writing, dressing, walking, climbing stairs, breathing, and other tasks. Each item is scored 0-4; a total of 48 represents full function. Rate of decline per month (ALSFRS-R slope) is the primary outcome measure in most ALS clinical trials.

faqs.question: When should ALS patients start non-invasive ventilation?
faqs.answer: Non-invasive ventilation (NIV/BiPAP) is recommended when forced vital capacity (FVC) falls below 50% of predicted, when SNIP falls below 40 cmH2O, or when symptoms of nocturnal hypoventilation appear (morning headache, somnolence, poor sleep). Earlier NIV initiation before FVC falls dramatically allows a smoother transition and better adaptation.

faqs.question: What is ALS frontotemporal dementia?
faqs.answer: ALS-frontotemporal dementia (ALS-FTD) occurs in approximately 15% of ALS patients, most commonly those with C9orf72 repeat expansion. It presents with personality change, apathy, disinhibition, and word-finding difficulty alongside motor neuron disease. ALS-FTD significantly complicates decision-making capacity, caregiving, and end-of-life planning.

faqs.question: What is voice banking in ALS?
faqs.answer: Voice banking is the process of recording a person's natural voice before significant dysarthria develops, creating a digital copy used in speech-generating devices. Patients speak thousands of phrases that are processed to generate a personalized synthetic voice. It is most effective when started early; organizations like ALS Association and SpeakUnique offer free voice banking services.

faqs.question: What is tofersen and who can receive it?
faqs.answer: Tofersen is an antisense oligonucleotide drug given by lumbar intrathecal injection monthly, approved by the FDA in 2023 for SOD1-mutant ALS. It reduces SOD1 protein production in motor neurons and significantly lowers neurofilament light chain, a biomarker of neurodegeneration. Only patients with a confirmed pathogenic SOD1 mutation are eligible.

faqs.question: Can ALS affect cognition and memory?
faqs.answer: Yes. Approximately 15% of ALS patients develop frontotemporal dementia (ALS-FTD), and a further 30-35% show milder cognitive or behavioral changes. Memory is generally preserved; the ALS-FTD pattern involves executive dysfunction, language difficulty, and personality change. Cognitive assessment is standard at diagnosis and repeated annually in specialist ALS clinics.

simpleName: ALS (Lou Gehrig's disease / motor neurone disease)
metaDescription: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis: progressive motor nerve disease causing muscle weakness. Symptoms, diagnosis and treatment of ALS reviewed by our medical board.
